variables.N: Number of turns in the coil
variables.B_axial: Magnetic field on the axial line (T)
variables.B_equatorial: Magnetic field on the equatorial line (T)
variables.tau: Torque on dipole in external field (N·m)

locked_facts: Magnetic dipole moment: M = NIA (for a coil of N turns, current I, area A)
Axial field of a magnetic dipole: B_axial = (μ₀/4π) × 2M/r³ (along the dipole axis)
Equatorial field of a magnetic dipole: B_equatorial = (μ₀/4π) × M/r³ (opposite to M direction)
B_axial = 2 × B_equatorial at the same distance r
Torque on a dipole in uniform field: τ = M × B = MB sin θ
Potential energy of dipole in uniform field: U = -M·B = -MB cos θ
A bar magnet is equivalent to a magnetic dipole — its field at large distances matches the dipole field pattern
truth_statements: A current loop acts as a magnetic dipole with moment M = NIA, directed along the axis of the loop (right-hand rule: curl fingers along current, thumb gives M direction)
The magnetic field of a dipole falls off as 1/r³ — much faster than the 1/r² of a monopole, because the dipole field arises from two equal and opposite poles
On the axial line, the field points in the same direction as M and is twice as strong as the equatorial field at the same distance
On the equatorial line, the field points opposite to M and has magnitude (μ₀/4π)(M/r³)
A bar magnet placed in a uniform magnetic field experiences a torque τ = MB sin θ that tends to align it along the field (like a compass needle)

aha_moment.what_to_show: A current loop and a bar magnet placed side by side. At large distances (r >> size), their field patterns are IDENTICAL — both show the 1/r³ dipole field. Field vectors on the axial line point along M, and on the equatorial line point opposite to M. A numerical readout confirms B_axial = 2 × B_equatorial at every distance r.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Split screen: current loop field lines on left, bar magnet field lines on right — they merge seamlessly at large r. The 2:1 ratio (axial:equatorial) is highlighted with colour-coded field vectors.
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students think bar magnets and current loops are fundamentally different — they see that at distances large compared to the size, both produce the exact same dipole field

simulation_states.label: Current loop as magnetic dipole
simulation_states.description: A circular current loop with current I and area A. The magnetic moment vector M = IA is shown along the axis (using right-hand rule). Field lines emerge from one face (north) and enter the other (south).
simulation_states.emphasis: A current loop IS a magnetic dipole. The moment M = NIA points along the axis — right-hand rule: curl fingers along current, thumb gives M direction.

simulation_states.label: Axial and equatorial fields
simulation_states.description: Field vectors shown at several points on the axial line (along M direction) and the equatorial line (perpendicular to M). Axial field vectors point along M, equatorial vectors point opposite to M. Numerical labels show B_axial = 2 × B_equatorial at each distance r.
simulation_states.emphasis: Axial: B = (μ₀/4π)(2M/r³), along M. Equatorial: B = (μ₀/4π)(M/r³), opposite to M. The factor of 2 is a key exam point.

simulation_states.label: Bar magnet ≡ magnetic dipole
simulation_states.description: A bar magnet appears next to the current loop. At large distances, their field lines overlap perfectly — both are dipole fields. The equivalence M = m × 2l (pole strength × magnetic length) is shown. Field vectors match at all far-field points.
simulation_states.emphasis: At large r, a bar magnet and a current loop are indistinguishable — both produce a dipole field with 1/r³ decay. B_axial = 2B_equatorial for both.

simulation_states.label: Interactive exploration
simulation_states.description: Student adjusts dipole moment M, distance r, and angle of observation (axial vs equatorial). Field magnitude updates in real time. A toggle places the dipole in an external field B to show torque τ = MB sin θ and potential energy U = -MB cos θ. Student can rotate the dipole to see torque variation.
simulation_states.emphasis: Move along axial vs equatorial line — see the 2:1 ratio. Increase r — watch field drop as 1/r³ (much faster than 1/r²). Place in external B — rotate to feel the torque aligning the dipole.

common_misconceptions.belief: The magnetic field of a bar magnet or dipole falls off as 1/r², just like a point charge
trigger_phrases: bar magnet ka field 1/r² se girega
common_misconceptions.correction: A magnetic dipole field falls off as 1/r³, NOT 1/r². This is because a dipole consists of two equal and opposite poles — the leading 1/r² terms from each pole partially cancel, leaving the weaker 1/r³ dependence. This is the same as for electric dipoles.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show field magnitude vs distance plot — the 1/r³ curve drops off much faster than 1/r². At double the distance, the field drops to 1/8 (not 1/4).

common_misconceptions.belief: The axial and equatorial fields of a dipole are equal in magnitude
trigger_phrases: dipole ka field har jagah same
common_misconceptions.correction: The axial field B_axial = (μ₀/4π)(2M/r³) is exactly TWICE the equatorial field B_equatorial = (μ₀/4π)(M/r³) at the same distance r. Moreover, they point in DIFFERENT directions — axial along M, equatorial opposite to M.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show field vectors at the same distance r on axial and equatorial lines — the axial arrow is twice as long as the equatorial arrow, and they point in opposite directions relative to M

common_misconceptions.belief: A bar magnet has isolated north and south monopoles that can be separated
trigger_phrases: ek pole alag kar sakte hain
common_misconceptions.correction: Magnetic monopoles have never been observed. If you cut a bar magnet in half, each piece becomes a complete dipole with its own north and south pole. This is fundamentally different from electric charges, where positive and negative charges can exist independently.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Animate cutting a bar magnet — each piece immediately develops both poles with field lines forming complete loops through each piece

legend.meaning: Torque = M × B = MB sin θ (N·m)
